• Cn3D: Good for a quick look!
• Chimera: Use for more sophisticated 
analysis and visualization

Next week: no class!!
From the syllabus:
“Assignment Part 2 (complete before class Sept. 23: a) Identify the set of structures for your 
protein of interest or whatever protein is (most) closely related to it.  Is there more than one 
structure? If so, do they differ in significant ways (different ligands, different conformational 
states, etc.)?  If so, what could be learned from this?   b) What relatives for this protein do you 
find in VAST?  c) Plot the hydrophobicity and charge distributions of your protein (or its closest 
relative) onto its 3D structure, and interpret what you see. 
Week 5:
Sept. 23, 25: 
Self-study and report preparation (Prof. Goodson will be at a 
conference). 
Students are not required to attend class this week, but are encourage to meet in class or 
elsewhere to discuss results and help each other out. 
Assignment: This week, review and update your assignments for the class up to this point.  
Write the results into a written report, which will serve as a first draft of the first part of your final 
project.  This report should be an extension of what you did last week and include an 
introduction that describes the protein and why you care about it. You should also carefully 
summarize (a) each analysis that you did; (b) why you did it; (c) how you did it (e.g., which 
database did you use?  Did you use defaults?); (d) what you saw; and (e) how you intepreted it. 
Using the “assignments” tab is Sakai, submit this draft report (along with any updated 
alignments) to Dr. Goodson by Friday September 27.  There will be a new tab (no need to write 
over your older files.
